stem: What is the correct order of the ML development cycle?

options.text: Train model → collect data → define problem → deploy
Define problem → collect data → train model → evaluate → deploy → monitor
Deploy → evaluate → train → collect data
Evaluate → train → define problem → deploy

explanation_after: The ML cycle always starts with problem definition, then data, then model building, evaluation, deployment, and ongoing monitoring. Skipping steps (especially problem definition) leads to building the wrong thing.

stem: A DummyClassifier in scikit-learn is used to:

options.text: Simulate user behaviour
Establish a baseline that simple heuristics can achieve, against which real models must be compared
Replace a model in production when it fails
Generate dummy training data

explanation_after: A DummyClassifier implements trivial strategies (always predict majority class, random prediction). If your real model can't beat it, something is fundamentally wrong with your data or approach.

stem: Experiment tracking (e.g. with MLflow) is important because:

options.text: It makes models train faster
It records hyperparameters, metrics, and artifacts so experiments are reproducible and comparable
It automatically tunes hyperparameters
It reduces overfitting

explanation_after: Without tracking, you lose the ability to reproduce a good run or understand what changed between experiments. Experiment tracking is table stakes for professional ML development.

stem: Data drift in a deployed model means:

options.text: The model's weights are gradually changing
The distribution of input data in production differs from the training distribution
Training data has become corrupted
The model is running slower over time

explanation_after: Data drift occurs when the real-world input distribution shifts from what was seen at training time. This causes model performance to degrade silently. Monitoring detects it; retraining fixes it.

stem: What is the purpose of a model registry?

options.text: To store training data
To track, version, and manage model artifacts through staging, production, and archival stages
To automatically retrain models on new data
To monitor inference latency

explanation_after: A model registry provides version control for trained models — who trained it, when, on what data, with what performance. It manages promotion from staging to production and enables rollbacks.

options.text: Deploying a model secretly without users knowing
Running a new model in parallel with the current production model, comparing outputs without affecting users
Training a model on shadowed (hidden) data
Deploying at night when traffic is low

explanation_after: Shadow deployment runs the new model alongside the production model. Real traffic goes to production; the new model's predictions are logged and compared. Only after validating performance is the new model promoted to production.
